prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. For this one-income household with two young children, I’d target **9 months of essential expenses**—more conservative than the usual 3–6 months because one adult currently provides all earned income. Owning the home outright lowers the monthly target, but repairs, property taxes, insurance, healthcare, childcare, food, utilities, and transportation still count.

**Recommended emergency fund**

| Essential household spending | 6-month floor | 9-month target | 12-month maximum |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000/month | $18,000 | **$27,000** | $36,000 |
| $4,000/month | $24,000 | **$36,000** | $48,000 |
| $5,000/month | $30,000 | **$45,000** | $60,000 |
| $6,000/month | $36,000 | **$54,000** | $72,000 |

Use this formula:

> **Emergency-fund target = monthly essential expenses × 9**

Keep a separate **home-repair reserve** rather than forcing major roof, HVAC, or plumbing costs into the emergency fund. A practical starting reserve is **1%–2% of the home’s value annually**, adjusted for the home’s age and condition.

Race and sex do not materially change this calculation. The important factors are one-income dependence, two young children, job stability, health-insurance exposure, and the absence of a mortgage. If employment is unusually secure and the spouse could return to work quickly, six months may suffice; if income is variable or benefits are weak, use 12 months.

I don’t have this family’s accounts or spending connected, so I can’t give one defensible dollar amount yet. The strongest recommendation is to total only essential monthly costs and fund **nine times that amount** in an insured, liquid savings or money-market account—not investments.

This is educational guidance, not individualized advice from a licensed financial advisor.
